reasoning: Hamas's core ideology and identity revolve around armed resistance, and it has consistently rejected any proposals to genuinely disarm. While some political figures have made hypothetical comments about laying down arms in the event of an independent Palestinian state along 1967 borders, these instances are explicitly conditional and would not qualify under the market's strict resolution criteria (which excludes plans contingent on future conditions). Even in the face of intense military pressure, Hamas is far more likely to agree to a ceasefire while retaining its weapons, go underground, or fight to the death than to issue a formal, official announcement of an acknowledged disarmament process. Given this deep-seated structural opposition to disarmament, the probability is set at the minimum allowable threshold for such highly unlikely scenarios.
